questions.text: I tend to spend money on experiences and tools that further my personal growth.
I hold the view that my calm is not something I construct — it's simply my natural state.
When I have extra money, my first thought is who else could use it.
I believe that preparation is a form of respect — for the task, for the people involved, and for myself.
I hold the view that moral engagement with suffering means letting it influence the trajectory of my decisions, not just the color of my emotions.
I believe that quick wins build momentum that eventually creates sustainable results.
I feel that spending on myself is only justified after everyone around me is taken care of.
I enjoy being assigned a role and doing it well.
I have been told that I seem unaffected by things that upset most people.
I notice when people adjust their vocabulary depending on who they are speaking to.
I experience emotions at a high volume — when something hits me, it hits hard.
I rarely miss a deadline because I build buffer time into my schedules.
I tend to direct the flow of conversation toward outcomes I envision.
I feel that the moral pull of another person's pain is the most honest signal I have about what I truly value.
My emotional peaks are dramatically higher than most people's seem to be.
I want to be the one who shapes how a project unfolds.
I find that I have to consciously remind myself to pay attention to social cues.
I detect when someone is using self-deprecating humor to preemptively manage others' impressions.
